author: Dr. Sarah Chen
hpi:
Mr. Morrison is a 71-year-old male with Type 2 DM on insulin and metformin, CKD Stage 3b, HFrEF, persistent A-fib on warfarin, and diabetic neuropathy who is being seen as part of his multi-day primary care visit series. Today's focus is diabetes management. His insulin glargine was recently increased from 24 to 26 units QHS by Dr. Adams (Internal Medicine) at his May visit. He reports his fasting blood sugars have improved from the 150-190 range down to 120-160 most mornings. Postprandial blood sugars remain somewhat elevated at 180-240, down from 200-280 previously. He has had no hypoglycemic episodes since the dose increase. He checks his blood sugars 4-5 times per week — fasting daily and postprandial 2-3 times. His most recent A1c was 7.8% three months ago and is being rechecked today.

He met with the diabetes educator in June and found the session helpful. He has been more consistent with carb counting, aiming for 45-60g per meal. He still struggles with evening snacking, particularly when watching television. He reports his wife has been preparing lower-carbohydrate meals. He denies polydipsia, polyuria beyond his baseline nocturia, or blurred vision changes. His neuropathy is managed on gabapentin 300-300-400mg with adequate control during the day but continued nighttime discomfort (rated 5/10 at night vs 3/10 during daytime).

His annual diabetic eye exam showed mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy without macular edema at his last ophthalmology visit (exact date per chart). Podiatry visit is scheduled for next month.

reviewOfSystems.Constitutional: Mild fatigue, weight stable at 97-98 kg, no fevers
reviewOfSystems.Cardiovascular: Baseline DOE, no chest pain, no palpitations
reviewOfSystems.GI: Appetite good, regular bowel movements, no nausea, no abdominal pain
reviewOfSystems.GU: Nocturia x2, no dysuria, no hematuria, no polyuria beyond baseline
reviewOfSystems.Musculoskeletal: Bilateral knee OA pain, managed. No new joint complaints
reviewOfSystems.Neurologic: Neuropathy as above — worse at night. No new weakness, no falls
reviewOfSystems.Endocrine: Blood sugars as detailed above. No hypoglycemia. No signs of thyroid dysfunction
reviewOfSystems.Eyes: No acute vision changes, no floaters. Mild NPDR per ophthalmology
reviewOfSystems.Skin: Dry skin bilateral lower extremities. No foot ulcers on self-inspection

physicalExam.General: Alert, oriented, obese male in no acute distress. Well-groomed, cooperative. BMI approximately 33
physicalExam.HEENT: Normocephalic. PERRL. Fundoscopic exam limited by patient cooperation — deferred to ophthalmology for detailed retinal assessment. Oropharynx clear. Dentition fair with mild gingivitis
physicalExam.Cardiovascular: Irregularly irregular at 72 bpm. S1/S2 normal. Known systolic murmur at apex. JVP not elevated
physicalExam.Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No adventitious sounds
physicalExam.Abdomen: Obese, soft, non-tender. No hepatomegaly. Normal bowel sounds. Insulin injection sites (bilateral abdomen) examined — no lipohypertrophy, no bruising, no induration
physicalExam.Extremities: Trace bilateral ankle edema. Feet examined: skin dry, intact without ulceration. Callus formation bilateral 5th metatarsal heads. Toenails mildly thickened bilaterally (onychomycosis). No interdigital maceration. Dorsalis pedis pulses 1+ bilaterally. Posterior tibial pulses barely palpable bilaterally
physicalExam.Neurologic: Decreased sensation to 10g monofilament: absent at bilateral great toes, 1st and 5th metatarsal heads; reduced at bilateral medial arches and heels. Vibration sense absent bilateral great toes, present but reduced at medial malleoli. Ankle reflexes absent. Proprioception reduced bilateral great toes

assessment.diagnosis: Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus with diabetic complications
assessment.notes: Glycemic control improving on glargine 26 units QHS and metformin 250mg BID. Fasting glucose improved to 120-160 (was 150-190). Postprandial still elevated at 180-240. A1c pending — if still above 7.5%, will need to consider intensification. Complications: (1) Neuropathy — progressing, with nocturnal worsening; (2) Nephropathy — CKD 3b, UACR 150; (3) Retinopathy — mild NPDR. Comprehensive diabetes management with attention to all microvascular endpoints.

Neuropathy exam shows progression — now with absent protective sensation at multiple sites bilateral feet (high risk for ulceration). Gabapentin titrated to 300-300-400mg with moderate symptom control. Nocturnal symptoms remain bothersome. Important to maintain close podiatry follow-up and reinforce foot care education given loss of protective sensation.
Per recent ophthalmology — mild nonproliferative diabetic retinopathy without macular edema. Surveillance in 6-12 months. Glycemic control and BP control are key modifiable factors for progression.

plan.action: A1c pending today. If A1c remains above 7.5% (likely given blood sugar patterns): (1) Continue insulin glargine 26 units QHS — fasting glucose improved, may not need further basal increase yet. (2) Consider adding rapid-acting insulin (lispro) at dinner — the largest meal and the meal most associated with postprandial spikes. Would start at 4 units before dinner, titrate up by 2 units every 3 days if 2-hour postprandial >180. Will discuss at next visit once A1c available. (3) Continue metformin 250mg BID. (4) SGLT2 inhibitor (empagliflozin) would provide modest additional glycemic benefit — ongoing discussion. (5) Dietary counseling — reinforce evening snacking avoidance, recommend protein-based snacks if needed. Diabetes educator follow-up in 2 months.

Loss of protective sensation confirmed on monofilament testing — patient is at high risk for diabetic foot ulcer. (1) Continue gabapentin 300-300-400mg. Nocturnal symptoms remain 5/10 — if not improving in 4 weeks, consider transition to pregabalin 50mg BID with bedtime dose of 75mg (renally adjusted). (2) Foot care education reinforced: inspect feet daily, wear shoes at all times including indoors, moisture cream to avoid cracking (not between toes), properly fitting shoes, no home callus removal. (3) Podiatry appointment confirmed for next month. (4) Custom orthotics discussed — may help redistribute pressure and reduce callus formation.

Mild NPDR — continue ophthalmology surveillance per their schedule (6-12 months). Key interventions: maintain best possible glycemic and BP control. A1c goal <7.5% given age and comorbidities. BP goal <130/80 — currently meeting this.

Will review A1c result when available — if above target, implement mealtime insulin plan. Continue multi-day visit series — SOB evaluation and CKD management visits upcoming. Next PCP comprehensive visit in 6-8 weeks.

attestation:
I personally saw and examined the patient. I agree with the documented findings and plan.

Electronically signed by: Dr. Sarah Chen
Date: 7/7/2023
